did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble classifier based on diverse Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin lesion classification. It employs deep learning architectures trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset of dermoscopic images to classify skin lesions into multiple classes (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, etc.).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing an ensemble classifier based on diverse Convolutional Neural Networks for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (data and classifier level diversity, Cohen's Kappa filtering), uses the ISIC 2018 dataset, and reports new experimental results (90.15% accuracy) comparing the proposed model against base networks and other classifiers.

title: An Ensemble Classifier Based on Diverse Convolutional Neural Networks for Skin Lesions Classification
brief_summary: This study develops an ensemble classifier based on diverse Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to improve the accuracy and robustness of skin lesion classification. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains over 13,000 dermoscopic images, to train, validate, and test their model. To enhance diversity, the approach employed data-level diversity through bootstrapping and classifier-level diversity by combining multiple CNN architectures and filtering out highly correlated models using Cohen’s Kappa score. The proposed ensemble classifier was evaluated against base CNNs and other competing classifiers. The main result showed that the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 90.15%, outperforming the baseline models in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven disease categories in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification. The paper notes that the ensemble delivers lower recall for the Melanoma class.
One of the seven disease categories in the ISIC 2018 dataset. The paper notes that Nevus samples are often misclassified into Basal cell carcinoma and Melanoma classes.

paper_terms: Benign keratosis (solar lentigo / seborrheic keratosis / lichen planus-like keratosis)
lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven disease categories in the ISIC 2018 dataset. The paper also refers to this class as 'seborrheic keratosis' in the context of the three-class subset used for specific ensemble construction.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis / Bowen’s disease, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven disease categories using an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (90.15%), precision, recall, F1-score, and Cohen's Kappa score. The task involves classifying images into specific lesion types rather than predicting continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The ISIC 2018 archive containing over 13,000 dermoscopic images collected from leading clinical centers internationally using various dermatoscope types. It is also referred to as the HAM10000 dataset in the context of its origin.
classes_used: Benign keratosis (solar lentigo / seborrheic keratosis / lichen planus-like keratosis)

use.training: Used to train the base Convolutional Neural Networks and the ensemble classifier.
use.testing: Used to test the proposed ensemble classifier and report performance metrics.
use.other: Used for 5-fold cross-validation to evaluate the method on the HAM10000 dataset (20% testing, 80% training per fold).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess inter-classifier agreement and filter out highly correlated base models to maximize ensemble diversity.
To create data-level diversity by sampling random subsets of training data for multiple training experiments.
To report the variability of accuracy across the base classifiers and the ensemble.
To evaluate the method's performance on the HAM10000 dataset (mentioned in brief summary and dataset use details).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics such as accuracy, standard deviation, and Cohen's Kappa score for diversity assessment.

ensemble_classifier.notes: Outperformed base convolutional neural networks and other competing classifiers in terms of accuracy and robustness.

ConvNext-Tiny.notes: Achieved the best accuracy among experimented backbone architectures.

EfficientNet-B0.notes: Demonstrated commendable mean accuracy; identified as the most efficient model (highest accuracy-to-parameter ratio).

SENet.notes: Performed a little weaker but had lower standard deviation of accuracy than ConvNext and EfficientNet.

DenseNet.notes: Performed a little weaker but had lower standard deviation of accuracy than ConvNext and EfficientNet.

ResNet.notes: Delivered the lowest mean accuracy on the ISIC dataset but exhibited low standard deviation.

per_class_metrics.notes: The model misclassified 20% of Melanoma samples as Basal cell carcinoma and Actinic keratosis. Actinic Keratosis was the second most challenging class, often misclassified into Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, and Nevus.

ensemble_composition.selection_criteria: Models with accuracy > 75% on test set and low Cohen's Kappa agreement (diversity filter) were selected from 50 training experiments per architecture.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of diverse Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using majority voting
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; models were initialized with Xavier initialization and trained from scratch on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

ensemble_composition.selection_criteria: Models with test accuracy >= 75% and low Cohen's Kappa agreement (diversity filter) were selected from 50 training experiments per architecture.

training_details.loss_function: Categorical Cross Entropy (CCE) with label smoothing (alpha = 0.1)
training_details.data_augmentation: Weighted bootstrapping, random affine transformations (rotations -180 to 180 degrees, translations 0.1, scale 0.7 to 1.3)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization by subtracting the mean of the ImageNet dataset from the images and dividing by the ImageNet dataset’s standard deviation
Center cropping to remove ineffective regions of dermoscopic images with a size of 240 × 240 pixels
Color jitter with random brightness and contrast variations (maximum variation set to 0.1)
Random affine transformations combining random rotations (-180 to 180 degrees), translations (0.1 in both directions), and scale variations (0.7 to 1.3)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during the training stage to increase dataset diversity.
Applied with a range of -30 to 30 degrees.
Applied with random brightness and contrast variations, with a maximum variation set to 0.1.
Combined random rotations (-180 to 180 degrees), translations (0.1 in both directions), and scale variations (ranging from 0.7 to 1.3).

Used to create data-level diversity by sampling random subsets of training data for multiple training experiments. Selection probability of each sample was weighted according to class distribution to mitigate class imbalance.
Mitigated class imbalance by sampling an equal number of samples for each class when constructing each batch during the training stage.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble classifier based on diverse Convolutional Neural Networks achieved an accuracy of 90.15% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming base convolutional neural networks and other competing classifiers in terms of accuracy and robustness.

Enhancing diversity at both the data level (via bootstrapping) and classifier level (via Cohen’s Kappa filtering to remove highly correlated models) significantly boosts ensemble accuracy and robustness.
Using Cohen’s Kappa score to filter out highly correlated base classifiers is an effective method for increasing ensemble diversity and improving performance.

The ensemble composition consisted of 25 diverse base models: 9 DenseNets, 7 ConvNexts, 5 EfficientNet-B0s, and 4 SEResNets, selected from 50 training experiments per architecture based on test accuracy (>75%) and low inter-classifier agreement.
The model demonstrated lower recall for the Melanoma and Basal cell carcinoma classes, with 20% of Melanoma samples being misclassified as Basal cell carcinoma or Actinic keratosis.
Among the base architectures tested, ConvNext-Tiny achieved the best accuracy, while EfficientNet-B0 was identified as the most efficient model in terms of the accuracy-to-parameter ratio.

The study highlights that maximizing diversity in ensemble deep neural networks is key to achieving high classification performance, particularly in addressing the challenges of inter-class similarity and intra-class variability in skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 (HAM10000) dataset, which may introduce population bias as the dataset's diversity might not fully represent the global population.

The ensemble exhibits lower recall for Melanoma and Basal cell carcinoma classes, with 20% of Melanoma samples being misclassified as Basal cell carcinoma or Actinic keratosis, which is a critical limitation for diagnostic safety.
The authors acknowledge that the number of samples is insufficient for deep learning approaches, particularly for classes like Nevus, Melanoma, and Actinic keratosis.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values); performance comparisons are based on descriptive statistics like accuracy and standard deviation.
The model was trained from scratch using Xavier initialization rather than leveraging pre-trained weights, which may impact generalization compared to transfer learning approaches.

While the ensemble outperforms base CNNs, the precision achieved is surpassed by a transformer-based architecture proposed in reference [47], indicating potential limitations of CNN-based ensembles relative to newer architectures.
The study did not evaluate the model on external datasets, limiting the assessment of its robustness and generalizability to unseen clinical data.
